description: Farming presets for Indonesian crops: soil-grown and hydroponic variants. Priority crops: shallot, garlic, celery, sweet potato, spring onion, chili. Additional: tomato, spinach, kangkung, lettuce, cucumber, eggplant, long bean, pakcoy. Nursery phases are defined separately in preset_nurseries.json. All DAP values are relative to planted_on (transplant date for crops with a nursery).

actions: Soak bulbs in fungicide solution (mancozeb) before planting
Apply mancozeb spray at 7 DAP if humidity is high
Monitor for bulb rot (Fusarium) — rogue infected plants immediately
Apply systemic insecticide (imidacloprid) if thrips exceed 5 per clump
Preventive contact fungicide every 5 days during rain
Remove and destroy wilting plants immediately
Stop insecticide spray 2 weeks before harvest
Apply protective fungicide against anthracnose
Reduce spray frequency during dry conditions
No specific pruning required for shallots
Manual weeding every 2 weeks
Remove yellowing or rotting leaves immediately

soil_parameters.notes: Gradually reduce irrigation to prevent bulb rot

growth_benchmarks.notes: Leaves should be fresh green
Swelling at stem base should be visible
Bulbs clearly formed, leaves starting to lodge — harvest approaching

fertilization_schedule.notes: Apply once at planting
Promote leaf growth
Stop nitrogen fertilizer 2 weeks before harvest

crops.name: Shallot (True Seed / TSS)
crops.notes: Grown from True Seed of Shallot (TSS). Nursery phase (20–25 days) is managed separately. DAP is relative to transplant date. Produces larger bulbs with higher yield than bulb-planted shallot.

actions: Apply mancozeb spray at 7 DAP if humidity is high
Monitor for thrips; apply imidacloprid at >5 insects per clump
Remove and destroy wilting transplants immediately
Apply systemic insecticide if thrips or caterpillar pressure increases
Preventive contact fungicide every 5–7 days during wet weather
Monitor for Alternaria leaf blight — spray copper fungicide at first sign
Stop insecticide spray 2 weeks before harvest
Apply protective fungicide against anthracnose
Gradually reduce irrigation to prevent bulb rot
No specific pruning required
Manual weeding every 2 weeks
Remove yellowing or rotting leaves immediately

soil_parameters.notes: Well-drained, fertile soil preferred; raised beds 20–30 cm high recommended
Reduce irrigation gradually to firm up bulbs; avoid waterlogging

growth_benchmarks.notes: Post-transplant establishment; active vegetative growth
Bulb swelling visible at stem base
TSS bulbs are typically larger than bulb-planted shallots; leaves lodging — harvest imminent

fertilization_schedule.notes: Apply once at transplanting into main field
Promote leaf and tiller growth after transplant
Stop nitrogen fertilizer 2 weeks before harvest; potassium for bulb sizing

actions: Treat seed cloves with fungicide (mancozeb / thiram) before planting
Monitor for Fusarium root rot — rogue infected plants immediately
Apply insecticide if early thrips attack detected
Spray protective fungicide every 5–7 days
Monitor for downy mildew during rain or high humidity
Apply contact insecticide if thrips population increases
Gradually reduce fungicide applications
Stop insecticide 3 weeks before harvest
Maintain air circulation between beds
No specific pruning required
Regular weeding every 2 weeks
Remove scapes (false flower stalks) promptly to redirect energy to cloves

soil_parameters.notes: Well-drained loamy soil preferred; avoid waterlogging which causes basal rot

growth_benchmarks.notes: Leaves should be dark green and erect
Maximum leaf canopy before scape removal
Leaves yellowing and lodging — harvest imminent

fertilization_schedule.notes: Apply once at planting; dolomite corrects pH and adds Mg & S
Push vegetative growth; sulfur from ZA benefits allium flavor
Switch to potassium-dominant nutrition for clove sizing

crops.name: Celery
crops.notes: Nursery phase (18–21 days) is managed separately. DAP is relative to transplant date.

actions: Apply protective fungicide (mancozeb) every 7 days if humidity is high
Monitor for early blight; spray copper-based fungicide at first symptoms
Use row covers or netting first 4–5 weeks to reduce pest pressure
Stop insecticide 2 weeks before harvest
Ensure calcium supply to prevent black heart disorder
Reduce overhead irrigation to limit fungal splash
Remove outer yellowing or damaged petioles to improve airflow
Tie stalks loosely if sprawling to keep upright form
Weed shallowly — celery has shallow roots, avoid deep cultivation
Bank soil or use paper sleeves around base to blanch stalks (optional for pale, tender stalks)
Remove any bolting flower stems immediately

soil_parameters.notes: Celery needs consistently moist, organically rich soil; drought causes stringy stalks

growth_benchmarks.notes: Post-transplant establishment; should show active new growth
Stalk development beginning; should be upright and compact
Harvest approaching; outer stalks ready when ≥30 cm

fertilization_schedule.notes: Incorporate into field before transplanting; high phosphorus supports root establishment
Nitrogen push to drive leaf & stalk mass
Calcium critical to prevent black heart; reduce nitrogen to avoid splitting

actions: Use vine tip cuttings (25 cm) from certified disease-free mother plants
Dip cuttings in fungicide solution before planting to prevent Fusarium
Apply soil insecticide (carbofuran) at planting to suppress wireworms and weevil larvae
Spray fenitrothion 0.5% at 30 DAP and again at 50 DAP for weevil control
Monitor for whitefly virus vectors; use yellow sticky traps
Hilling at 25–30 DAP covers developing roots, reducing weevil entry
Final fenitrothion spray at 70 DAP if weevil pressure remains high
Stop all insecticide 3 weeks before harvest
Reduce irrigation to harden roots before harvest
Lift and redirect vines every 2–3 weeks to prevent adventitious rooting away from ridge
Cut and remove excess vines to maintain airflow and reduce disease
Weed thoroughly at 10–12 DAP and again at 25–30 DAP

pests.name: Sweet potato weevil (Cylas formicarius)
pests.action_threshold: adult weevils or entry tunnels in vines found

pests.name: Fusarium stem rot (Fusarium solani)
pests.action_threshold: internal browning of vine base found

pests.name: Sweet potato weevil (Cylas formicarius)
pests.action_threshold: tunneling found in >2% of sampled roots

soil_parameters.notes: Light, well-drained ridged soil (30–40 cm high) maximizes root yield and minimizes weevil entry

growth_benchmarks.notes: Cutting should have rooted and begun active vine growth
Soil surface should show early root swelling near crown
Roots should be uniform in shape; check by sampling before full harvest

fertilization_schedule.notes: Apply in furrows at planting; avoid excessive nitrogen which promotes vines over roots
Phosphorus and potassium encourage root initiation
Potassium-heavy side-dress at bulking stage; do NOT add nitrogen

actions: Treat seeds or seedling roots with fungicide (thiram) before transplanting
Apply mulch to maintain soil moisture and suppress weeds
Monitor for damping-off in nursery; reduce watering if stems thin at base
Spray protective fungicide (mancozeb) every 7 days in wet weather
Monitor for thrips; apply insecticide (spinosad or imidacloprid) at threshold
Ensure good drainage to prevent purple blotch
Weed manually every 10–14 days; spring onions compete poorly with weeds
Remove dead or yellowing outer leaves to encourage new growth
No topping required unless harvesting leaf portion selectively

pests.name: Downy mildew (Peronospora destructor)
pests.action_threshold: pale green/yellow patches with gray fuzz found

soil_parameters.notes: Loamy soil with good drainage; avoid clay-heavy soils

growth_benchmarks.notes: Transplant shock recovery complete; active new leaf growth
Rapid leaf elongation phase
Ready for first selective harvest of outer leaves

fertilization_schedule.notes: Apply before transplanting; mix well into soil
Nitrogen-focused to drive fast leaf elongation
Balanced nutrition; stop 1 week before harvest

crops.name: Chili Pepper
crops.notes: Nursery phase (21–25 days) is managed separately. DAP is relative to transplant date.

actions: Dip transplant roots in Trichoderma + Imidacloprid 0.5 ml/L for 15 min before transplanting
Apply systemic insecticide (imidacloprid) if thrips or virus vectors detected
Protective fungicide every 5–7 days in wet weather
Rotate contact and systemic fungicides to prevent resistance
Monitor for yellow leaf curl virus (Begomovirus); remove infected plants
Use selective insecticides to preserve beneficial insects
Stop insecticide 7 days before harvest
Continue protective fungicide against anthracnose
Harvest regularly every 3–5 days to maintain plant productivity
Remove all suckers below the first V-shaped branching
Strip lower leaves up to 20 cm from ground to improve airflow
Remove non-productive lateral branches
Remove first-set fruits (too early) to strengthen the main stem
Optional topping for overly tall plants
Remove diseased or dried branches immediately
Remove rotting fruits on the plant to prevent spread

pests.name: Fruit fly (Bactrocera sp.)
pests.action_threshold: dropped fruits or small entry holes found

soil_parameters.notes: Maintain calcium to prevent BER; avoid drought stress during fruit set

growth_benchmarks.notes: First flowers appearing
First fruits ready to pick

fertilization_schedule.notes: Apply once at transplanting
Promote vegetative growth
Boron critical for fruit set
Calcium prevents blossom end rot
Stop nitrogen; focus on potassium for heat level and shelf life

crops.name: Tomato
crops.notes: Nursery phase (18–21 days) is managed separately. DAP is relative to transplant date.

actions: Spray protective fungicide (chlorothalonil / mancozeb) every 7 days
Apply systemic insecticide if aphids or whiteflies detected
Use mulch to reduce nematode pressure and splash infection
Rotate fungicides to combat early and late blight
Stop insecticide 7 days before harvest
Monitor for TYLCV (tomato yellow leaf curl); remove infected plants
Remove suckers below first flower truss weekly for indeterminate types
Train main stem to single or double leader along vertical twine
Strip lower leaves up to 30 cm from ground after transplanting
Remove lower leaves as fruit sets to improve airflow
Top the plant at desired height after 6–8 trusses (indeterminate)
Remove all diseased or cracked fruits promptly

pests.name: Early blight (Alternaria solani)
pests.action_threshold: >5% of leaves with concentric ring spots

pests.name: Late blight (Phytophthora infestans)
pests.action_threshold: water-soaked lesions with white mold found

pests.name: BER (Blossom end rot — Ca deficiency)
pests.action_threshold: dark sunken patches on fruit base found

soil_parameters.notes: Consistent soil moisture prevents BER and fruit cracking

growth_benchmarks.notes: Active flowering
Green fruit swelling; first fruits ripening

fertilization_schedule.notes: Apply once at transplanting
Build canopy; phosphorus supports root development
Boron for fruit set; calcium prevents BER
High-K nutrition for color, firmness and shelf life

actions: Avoid overhead irrigation in dense stands to reduce downy mildew risk
Apply light neem oil spray if caterpillar pressure develops
Rotate planting blocks to avoid soilborne disease buildup
Thin seedlings to 5–10 cm spacing at 10 DAP for airflow
Remove bolting plants immediately to prevent premature seed set
Selective outer-leaf harvest can extend production period

pests.name: Downy mildew (Peronospora farinosa)
pests.action_threshold: yellow patches with white fuzz found

soil_parameters.notes: Rich in organic matter; consistent moisture for tender leaves

growth_benchmarks.notes: Thinning stage
Baby leaf harvest possible from this point

fertilization_schedule.notes: Mix into soil before direct seeding
Light nitrogen top-dress for rapid leaf growth

actions: Monitor for aphid colonies on young shoots; spray neem oil at first sign
Avoid waterlogging in raised bed cultivation to prevent root rot
Keep field sanitation high — remove dead plant material promptly
Harvest by cutting stems 5 cm above soil; plant will ratoon for multiple cuts
Thin seedlings at 7 DAP if broadcast-seeded
Remove flowering stems if found to maintain leaf quality

soil_parameters.notes: Tolerates flooded conditions; ideal for lowland tropical Indonesia

growth_benchmarks.notes: Rapid growth should be evident; leaves dark green
First harvest; cut stems above lowest node

fertilization_schedule.notes: Broadcast before seeding or transplanting
Nitrogen-dominant for rapid leaf mass

actions: Sanitize NFT/DFT channels between batches with diluted bleach (1%)
Monitor nutrient solution for root rot (brown slimy roots) — increase aeration
Flush and replace nutrient solution weekly to prevent salt buildup
Harvest by cutting above lowest node for ratoon production
Remove any yellowing leaves promptly to prevent nutrient solution contamination

solution_parameters.notes: Low EC to avoid seedling stress; use half-strength AB Mix
Full-strength AB Mix; check EC daily and top up with fresh solution

growth_benchmarks.notes: Roots should be white and dense in channel
Ready for harvest

nutrient_schedule.notes: Use rockwool or cocopeat plugs in net pots
Refresh solution fully every 7 days; top up daily with plain water

actions: Avoid planting in full midday tropical sun; use 30–50% shade netting
Keep soil moisture consistent to prevent tip-burn (Ca deficiency)
Apply copper-based fungicide if downy mildew signs appear
Remove outer damaged or yellowing leaves to improve appearance
No pruning required; harvest whole plant at maturity
Thin to 20–25 cm spacing at 14 DAP if direct-seeded

soil_parameters.notes: Loose, well-drained, organically rich soil; sensitive to heat — use shade cloth in lowland tropics

growth_benchmarks.notes: Should show active rosette expansion
Ready to harvest; head should feel firm when pressed

fertilization_schedule.notes: Incorporate before transplanting
Calcium nitrate prevents tipburn; avoid excess nitrogen which causes loose heads

actions: Maintain adequate air circulation around leaves to prevent tipburn and fungal issues
Keep water temperature below 28°C to discourage root rot pathogens
Sanitize channels and reservoirs thoroughly between batches
Remove dead or yellowing outer leaves to prevent contaminating the nutrient solution
No pruning required; harvest whole plant

solution_parameters.notes: Half-strength AB Mix; target pH 6.0 for optimal calcium uptake
Full-strength AB Mix; maintain calcium ratio to prevent tipburn

growth_benchmarks.notes: Roots should be long, white, and bushy
Head forming; leaves should be bright green without burn

nutrient_schedule.notes: Use rockwool or cocopeat media plugs
Replace solution weekly; add calcium supplement if tipburn persists

actions: Use fine mesh netting to exclude caterpillar moths
Apply Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) spray for caterpillar control (organic-safe)
Monitor for black rot; remove infected plants and avoid overhead irrigation
Thin seedlings at 10 DAP to 15–20 cm spacing
Remove yellowing outer leaves
Harvest whole plant at maturity or take outer leaves progressively

pests.name: Diamondback moth (Plutella xylostella)
pests.action_threshold: larvae or window-pane feeding damage found

soil_parameters.notes: Fast-growing brassica; consistent moisture is key to prevent bolting

growth_benchmarks.notes: Active leaf expansion; should show dark green color
Ready for harvest; heads compact and firm

fertilization_schedule.notes: Incorporate before transplanting
Nitrogen dominant; calcium for cell integrity

actions: Use fine mesh or net house to exclude Plutella moths and aphids
Sanitize growing channels between batches
Monitor roots for Pythium — increase DO (dissolved oxygen) if detected
Remove yellow or dead leaves to avoid contaminating nutrient solution
Harvest whole plant; no selective pruning required

solution_parameters.notes: Germinate in rockwool; transfer at 2-true-leaf stage
Full AB Mix; refresh weekly; higher EC for dense heads

growth_benchmarks.notes: Roots white and vigorous in channels
Head compact and dark green; ready to harvest

nutrient_schedule.notes: Replace solution weekly; top up daily with plain water

actions: Treat seeds with Thiram or Captan fungicide before planting
Apply preventive fungicide (mancozeb) every 7 days
Monitor for downy mildew; improve air circulation between rows
Rotate fungicides to cover downy and powdery mildew
Stop insecticide 7 days before harvest
Use protein bait traps for fruit fly (Bactrocera sp.)
Train main stem along vertical string or bamboo trellis
Remove lateral shoots below node 5–7 to reduce crowding
Pinch growing tip after desired stem height to encourage laterals for more fruit
Remove old and yellowing leaves to improve light penetration
Harvest fruit every 2–3 days to prevent over-maturity and extend plant productivity

pests.name: Downy mildew (Pseudoperonospora cubensis)
pests.action_threshold: >5% of leaves with angular yellow patches

pests.name: Fruit fly (Bactrocera cucurbitae)
pests.action_threshold: oviposition stings or maggots in fruit found

soil_parameters.notes: Sandy-loam preferred; avoid heavy clay and salinity above 2.5 mS/cm

growth_benchmarks.notes: Tendrils forming; begin training to trellis
First flowers should appear
Fruit swelling; harvest when fruit is dark green and firm

fertilization_schedule.notes: Apply once at planting
Push canopy growth before flowering
Boron critical for fruit set; K for quality

crops.name: Eggplant
crops.notes: Nursery phase (21–25 days) is managed separately. DAP is relative to transplant date.

actions: Apply systemic insecticide (emamectin benzoate) at 10 DAP against EFSB
Collect and destroy EFSB-infested shoots weekly
Spray protective fungicide (copper-based) every 7 days
Continue EFSB monitoring; destroy infested fruits weekly
Stop insecticide 7 days before harvest
Rotate fungicides to manage Phomopsis blight on fruit
Stake and train plants; remove suckers below first branching
Strip lower leaves up to 25 cm from ground for airflow
Remove non-productive lateral branches to concentrate energy
Remove all infested or diseased fruits immediately

pests.name: EFSB — Eggplant Fruit & Shoot Borer (Leucinodes orbonalis)
Phomopsis blight (Phomopsis vexans)
pests.action_threshold: dark fruit lesions or stem narrowing found

pests.name: Fruit fly (Bactrocera dorsalis)
pests.action_threshold: oviposition stings or soft spots found

soil_parameters.notes: Avoid planting after solanaceous crops (tomato, chili, potato) to reduce wilt risk

growth_benchmarks.notes: First flowers should appear
First fruits ready to harvest when skin is still glossy

fertilization_schedule.notes: Apply once at transplanting
Build strong vegetative framework before flowering
Boron for fruit set; reduce nitrogen
High potassium for fruit quality and glossy skin

actions: Treat seeds with Rhizobium inoculant to promote nitrogen fixation
Apply soil insecticide (carbofuran) at planting against bean fly (Ophiomyia sp.)
Monitor for early damping-off; ensure good drainage
Spray insecticide (lambda-cyhalothrin) at flowering to protect against pod borer
Apply preventive fungicide (mancozeb) during wet weather
Use pheromone traps for pod borer monitoring
Stop insecticide 7 days before harvest
Continue monitoring for pod borer (Maruca vitrata)
Harvest pods every 3–4 days at tender stage
Train vines to 1.5–2 m bamboo trellis or string
Remove lower leaves touching soil to reduce fungal splash
Pinch growing tip when vine exceeds trellis height

pests.action_threshold: flower/pod webbing or entry holes found

pests.name: Bean fly (Ophiomyia phaseoli)
pests.action_threshold: blister trails or collapsed seedlings found

pests.action_threshold: white powdery patches on >10% of canopy

soil_parameters.notes: Nitrogen-fixing legume; avoid heavy nitrogen at planting — let Rhizobium do the work

growth_benchmarks.notes: Vine should be reaching trellis; tendrils forming
Flowering should begin
Pods at harvest length; harvest before seeds bulge visibly

Phosphorus and potassium; avoid excess nitrogen to encourage pod production
